result.markdown:
---
title: "Losses"
---







The Bregman-Riesz loss generalizes the squared Riesz loss via a strictly convex potential $\phi$. `rieszreg` ships four built-in `LossSpec` classes; backends consume per-row gradient and Hessian sourced from the loss spec.

::: {.callout-note title="Backend support"}
- **Boosting** ([`rieszboost`](backends/boosting.qmd)): all four losses.
- **Kernel ridge** ([`krrr`](backends/kernel.qmd)): `SquaredLoss` only â€” non-quadratic losses need a Newton iteration on the kernel system.
- **Random forest, augmentation-style** (`forestriesz.AugForestRieszRegressor`): all four losses. Tree structure from the squared MSE criterion, then a per-leaf Newton replaces each leaf's stored Î¸ with the Bregman optimum.
- **Random forest, moment-style** (`forestriesz.ForestRieszRegressor`): `SquaredLoss` only â€” the moment-style per-leaf gradient is not exposed by the loss API.
- **Neural network** ([`riesznet`](backends/neural.qmd)): all four losses. Each loss's per-row Bregman form is implemented in autograd-friendly torch ops; gradient parity vs the analytic loss is checked at 1e-8.

Pass an unsupported loss and the backend raises at fit time with a clear error.
:::

## Loss table

| Loss | Potential $\phi$ | Link $\eta \to \alpha$ | Domain | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SquaredLoss` | $t^2$ | identity | $\mathbb{R}$ | Default. |
| `KLLoss` | $t \log t$ | exp | $(0, \infty)$ | Density-ratio targets (TSM, IPSI). Guarantees $\hat\alpha > 0$. Requires non-negative $m$-coefficients. |
| `BernoulliLoss` | $t \log t + (1-t)\log(1-t)$ | sigmoid | $(0, 1)$ | $\alpha_0$ known to lie in $(0, 1)$. Same coefficient-sign restriction as `KLLoss`. |
| `BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi)` | $t^2$ + sigmoid-scaled link | $\mathrm{lo} + (\mathrm{hi}-\mathrm{lo}) \cdot \sigma$ | $(\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})$ | Squared-loss fit with prior bounds (e.g. trimmed-propensity ratios). |

`SquaredLoss` is the standard Lee-Schuler / Chernozhukov objective. Per-row loss in $\alpha$-space: $a \cdot \alpha^2 + b \cdot \alpha$.

## `KLLoss` for density-ratio targets

`KLLoss` ($\phi = t \log t$, exp link) targets density ratios. The exponential link keeps $\hat\alpha > 0$.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python




::: {.cell}

```{.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
from rieszboost import RieszBooster
from rieszreg import KLLoss, TSM

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 1000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
pi = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a = rng.binomial(1, pi)
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": a.astype(float), "x": x})

booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=KLLoss(max_eta=50),
    n_estimators=300,
    learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=3,
).fit(df)

alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"min Î±Ì‚ = {alpha_hat.min():.4f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
min Î±Ì‚ = 0.1174
```


:::

```{.python .cell-code}
print(f"mean Î±Ì‚ on treated rows = {alpha_hat[a == 1].mean():.3f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
mean Î±Ì‚ on treated rows = 1.845
```


:::
:::




## R




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(0)
n  <- 1000
x  <- runif(n)
pi <- 1 / (1 + exp(-(8 * x - 4)))
a  <- rbinom(n, 1, pi)
df <- data.frame(a = as.numeric(a), x = x)

booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = KLLoss(max_eta = 50),
  n_estimators = 300L,
  learning_rate = 0.05,
  max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("min alpha_hat = %.4f\n", min(alpha_hat)))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
min alpha_hat = 0.1173
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat(sprintf("mean alpha_hat on treated rows = %.3f\n",
            mean(alpha_hat[a == 1])))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
mean alpha_hat on treated rows = 1.867
```


:::
:::




:::

`KLLoss` requires all augmented `b` coefficients $\le 0$, equivalent to all $m$-coefficients being non-negative. The check happens at fit time and raises with a clear error if violated.

::: {.callout-warning title="When KLLoss can be unstable"}
Pure-counterfactual rows ($a=0$, $b<0$) have no minimum in $\eta$ under KL â€” leaf weights at low-overlap points can extrapolate. Use `SquaredLoss` unless the positivity guarantee is needed.
:::

## `BoundedSquaredLoss` for hard prior bounds

If you know $\alpha_0 \in [\text{lo}, \text{hi}]$ (e.g. you've trimmed propensities to $[\varepsilon, 1-\varepsilon]$ so $1/\pi \in [1, 1/\varepsilon]$), `BoundedSquaredLoss(lo, hi)` keeps predictions in that box via a sigmoid-scaled link. The per-row loss in $\alpha$-space matches `SquaredLoss`; the link reparameterizes $\eta$ so $\hat\alpha$ saturates at the bounds rather than extrapolating past them.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python




::: {.cell}

```{.python .cell-code}
from rieszreg import BoundedSquaredLoss
booster = RieszBooster(
    estimand=TSM(level=1),
    loss=BoundedSquaredLoss(lo=0.0, hi=12.0),
    n_estimators=200, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=3,
).fit(df)
alpha_hat = booster.predict(df)
print(f"alpha_hat in ({alpha_hat.min():.3f}, {alpha_hat.max():.3f})")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
alpha_hat in (0.182, 11.591)
```


:::
:::




## R




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
booster <- RieszBooster$new(
  estimand = TSM(level = 1),
  loss = BoundedSquaredLoss(lo = 0.0, hi = 12.0),
  n_estimators = 200L, learning_rate = 0.05, max_depth = 3L
)
booster$fit(df)
alpha_hat <- booster$predict(df)
cat(sprintf("alpha_hat in (%.3f, %.3f)\n",
            min(alpha_hat), max(alpha_hat)))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
alpha_hat in (0.204, 11.784)
```


:::
:::




:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Pick bounds tightly"}
Generous bounds make the sigmoid link saturate over most of $\eta$, so boosting steps barely move predictions. Set `lo` and `hi` to your prior on $\alpha_0$.
:::

`BernoulliLoss` is the same construction with $\text{lo}=0$, $\text{hi}=1$ and the Bernoulli Bregman potential. Use it when $\alpha_0$ is a probability.

## Initialization

Every `RieszEstimator` subclass takes an `init=` constructor arg that controls the Î±-space starting value of the model. The orchestrator converts `init` to an Î·-space `base_score` via `loss.alpha_to_eta`; backends use `base_score` as a prediction offset (boosting starts the additive sum from there, neural backends add it inside the forward pass, kernel-ridge folds it into the linear-system right-hand side, forests add it to the leaf prediction).

The default â€” `init=None` â€” sets Î± to the **empirical loss-minimizing constant**.

For any Bregman loss with strictly convex $\phi$, the constant $a^*$ that minimizes the per-row Riesz loss
$$
P_n L(a) \;=\; \psi(a) \;-\; \varphi'(a) \cdot \bar m, \qquad \bar m = P_n[m(Z, 1)]
$$
satisfies the FOC $\psi'(a) = \varphi''(a) \bar m$. Using $\psi(t) = t \varphi'(t) - \varphi(t)$ we get $\psi'(t) = t \varphi''(t)$, so the FOC collapses to $a^* = \bar m$. The result is **loss-independent** â€” the only loss-specific step is projecting $\bar m$ into the loss's Î±-domain when its codomain is restricted (KL: $\alpha > 0$; Bernoulli: $\alpha \in (0,1)$; BoundedSquared: $\alpha \in (\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})$). Each `LossSpec` exposes `best_constant_init(m_bar)` for that projection.

For the built-in estimands $\bar m$ is structurally constant (doesn't depend on the data realization):

| Estimand | $m(z, 1)$ per row | $\bar m$ |
|---|---|---|
| `ATE`, `ATT`, `AdditiveShift`, `LocalShift` | $1 + (-1) = 0$ | $0$ |
| `TSM(v)` | $1$ | $1$ |
| `StochasticIntervention` | $K \cdot (1/K) = 1$ | $1$ |

For custom estimands $\bar m$ can depend on the data; the orchestrator computes it from the training rows after the validation split (no peeking) by tracing each row and averaging the sum of trace coefficients.

Override the default by passing a float:

```python
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=ATE(), init=0.0)        # explicit zero baseline
booster = RieszBooster(estimand=TSM(level=1), init=2.5) # informed prior
```

::: {.callout-note title="Boundary cases"}
For `BernoulliLoss` + `TSM` (or any density-ratio estimand with $\bar m = 1$) the loss-minimizing constant sits on the upper boundary, and the projection clips to $1 - \varepsilon$. The sigmoid is saturated at that init and gradients are tiny, so iterative training may stall. Pass `init=0.5` (or another interior value) when this matters. The `KLLoss` / `BoundedSquaredLoss` boundaries can hit the same issue when $\bar m$ lands exactly on a bound.
:::

## Custom losses

::: {.callout-note title="Python-only"}
Custom `LossSpec` subclasses are Python-only. From R, define the subclass in Python via `reticulate::py_run_string` and pass the instance via `loss=`.
:::

Implement the `LossSpec` protocol from `rieszreg.losses`:

```python
class LossSpec(Protocol):
    name: str

    def link_to_alpha(self, eta: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Inverse link: convert backend output Î· to Î±."""
    def alpha_to_eta(self, alpha):
        """Forward link: convert Î± to Î· (used for `init=` translation)."""
    def loss_row(self, a, b, alpha):
        """Per-row loss in Î± space: aÂ·Ïˆ(Î±) + (b/2)Â·Ï†'(Î±)."""
    def gradient(self, a, b, eta):
        """âˆ‚loss_row/âˆ‚Î·, using Î± = link_to_alpha(Î·)."""
    def hessian(self, a, b, eta, hessian_floor):
        """âˆ‚Â²loss_row/âˆ‚Î·Â² (floored)."""
    def best_constant_init(self, m_bar: float) -> float:
        """Loss-minimizing constant Î±* given mÌ„ = E[m(Z, 1)]."""
    def validate_coefficients(self, b: np.ndarray) -> None:
        """Raise if (a, b) violate this loss's domain."""
```

[`SquaredLoss`](https://github.com/rieszreg/rieszreg/blob/main/rieszreg/python/rieszreg/losses/squared.py) and [`KLLoss`](https://github.com/rieszreg/rieszreg/blob/main/rieszreg/python/rieszreg/losses/kl.py) are short reference implementations.

The Bregman extension follows Hines & Miles ([2510.16127](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.16127)) and Kato ([2601.07752](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.07752)).
